question_text: A chef has eight identical-looking pastries, and one will be slightly more or less dense than the rest due to a mistake in the recipe. How can the chef identify the denser or less dense pastry in only two uses of a kitchen scale?
participant_answer: Divide the pastries into three groups of three, three, and two. Weigh the two groups of three pastries against each other. If one side is heavier, then the denser or less dense pastry is in that group. If the two sides are equal, then the denser or less dense pastry is in the group of two. Take the group that contains the denser or less dense pastry and weigh two of the pastries against each other. If one is heavier, then that is the denser pastry. If they are equal, then the remaining pastry is the denser or less dense pastry.
